questions.question: Which function returns the current working directory as a string?
questions.explanation: os.getcwd() returns the current working directory as a string. os.curdir is a constant (typically '.'), not a function, and sys has no getcwd().

questions.question: What does sys.path represent in Python?
choices: The file system path of the running script
The list of directories Python searches when importing modules
The PATH environment variable as a list
The path to the Python interpreter executable
questions.explanation: sys.path is the module search path -- a list of directory strings that Python checks in order when you use an import statement. It can be modified at runtime.

questions.question: How do you list the contents of a directory using the os module?
questions.explanation: os.listdir(path) returns a list of names (strings) of the entries in the given directory. os.scandir() also works but returns DirEntry objects, not a plain list.

questions.question: Which attribute holds the command-line arguments passed to a Python script?
questions.explanation: sys.argv is a list of strings where sys.argv[0] is the script name and sys.argv[1:] are the arguments passed on the command line.

questions.question: The os.___ function changes the current working directory to the specified path.
questions.explanation: os.chdir(path) changes the current working directory of the process to the given path. It is the counterpart to os.getcwd().

questions.question: The ___ attribute of the sys module contains the list of directories Python searches for modules during import.
questions.explanation: sys.path is initialized from PYTHONPATH and installation-dependent defaults. You can append directories to it at runtime to make additional modules importable.
